title: Butter on Linen
timeEstimate: 15–30 minutes; structured / lined / dry-clean-only linen escalates to a pro
stainChemistry: Butter is an oil/fat-family stain. Oil-based stains require a surfactant (liquid laundry detergent) to emulsify and lift the fat from the fiber. Linen is a cellulose (flax) fiber; it can tolerate controlled, conservative laundering when the care label allows, but colored linen, structured pieces, and lined / embellished garments need extra caution and often a professional dry cleaner.
whyThisWorks: A small amount of liquid laundry detergent provides surfactant action to break up butterfat. Cool or lukewarm water (per care label) avoids unnecessary heat exposure. Air-drying and inspecting before any heat keeps residual oil from being baked into the fiber.
defaultAssumption: Simple flat linen (napkin, plain shirt) on a washable care label is a Home candidate. Structured, lined, embellished, vintage, dry-clean-only, or unstable-dye linen is a professional case from the start.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Read the care label and inspect the garment construction. Structured, lined, embellished, dry-clean-only, vintage, or unstable-dye linen routes to the professional path and stops here.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Reject too-soiled or non-colorfast linen per care-label guidance. Do not proceed if the care label disallows wet treatment.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Use a dull edge to gently lift any solid butter off the linen. Do not rub or press into the weave.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Minimal mechanical action only. Aggressive rubbing can spread oil or damage the linen weave.

spottingProtocol.agent: Colorfastness test on a hidden seam
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dab a small amount of liquid laundry detergent and cool water on an inside seam using a clean white cloth. Press for one minute and check the cloth for color transfer.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Stop if dye transfers. Colored or unstable-dye linen routes to the professional path.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply a small amount of liquid laundry detergent directly to the stain. Work it in lightly with a fingertip or soft cloth.
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: Brief dwell per product label, then rinse cool
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Follow the detergent product label. Do not mix cleaning agents.

spottingProtocol.agent: Launder per care label, cool or lukewarm only
spottingProtocol.instruction: Wash on the cool or lukewarm setting permitted by the care label. Rinse thoroughly.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Do not exceed the temperature limit on the care label; controlled temperatures protect the linen.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Lay flat to air-dry and inspect for residual oil before any heat. If oil remains, repeat the pretreat and laundering step; do not tumble-dry until the stain is fully gone.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Heat applied to residual oil sets the stain into the fiber.

steps: 1. Inspect fiber, dye stability, construction, and care label before any wet treatment.
2. Lift excess oil with a dull edge; minimal mechanical action.
3. Apply surfactant pretreat appropriate to the oil family; rinse and assess.
4. Launder cool or lukewarm per the care label; do not exceed.

5. Air-dry flat and inspect before any heat. Escalation to controlled oxidizer chemistry only when the care label permits, colorfastness is confirmed, and PPE / ventilation per product SDS are in place.
products: Cool / lukewarm water per care label
professionalProtocol.temperature: Cool to lukewarm per care label; no heat on residual oil
warnings: Use cool or lukewarm water unless the care label specifically permits warmer washing; avoid hot water in the Home path for uncertain, colored, structured, or high-value linen
Never tumble-dry linen with residual oil — heat sets the stain
Never apply chlorine bleach on colored linen
Structured / lined / embellished / dry-clean-only / vintage linen is a professional case from the start
Pro chemistry beyond consumer detergent requires SDS-driven PPE and ventilation

homeSolutions.agent: Care Label and Construction Check First
homeSolutions.instruction: Read the care label. If it says dry-clean-only, or the linen is structured, lined, embellished, or vintage, stop and take it to a professional dry cleaner. Simple flat linen (napkin, plain shirt) on a washable care label can continue.
Use a dull butter knife or spoon edge to gently lift any solid butter off the linen. Do not rub — that pushes oil into the weave.

On colored linen, dab a little liquid laundry detergent and cool water on an inside seam with a clean white cloth. Press one minute. If color transfers to the cloth, stop and take to a professional. White linen can skip this step.
Put a small amount of mild liquid laundry detergent directly on the stain. Work it in gently with your fingertip or a soft cloth — do not scrub.

homeSolutions.agent: Cool or Lukewarm Wash Per Care Label
homeSolutions.instruction: Wash the linen on the cool or lukewarm setting your care label allows. Rinse thoroughly. Use cool or lukewarm water unless the care label specifically permits warmer washing. Avoid hot water for uncertain, colored, structured, or high-value linen.
Lay the linen flat to air-dry and look closely for any remaining oily mark before any heat. If a stain remains, repeat steps 4 and 5 once. Do not tumble-dry until the stain is fully gone.

If the stain persists after two careful passes, or if it has already been through a hot wash or dryer, take it to a professional dry cleaner. Tell them: "Butter on [simple / colored] linen, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with liquid laundry detergent and a cool care-label wash, air-dried flat."

steps: 1. Read care label and construction; escalate dry-clean-only / structured / lined / embellished / vintage to a pro.
2. Lift excess butter gently with a dull edge — do not rub.
3. On colored linen, hidden-area colorfastness test before detergent.
4. Apply a small amount of liquid laundry detergent.
5. Cool or lukewarm wash per care label — no hot water on linen.
6. Air-dry flat and inspect for residual oil before any heat.
7. Repeat once if needed; escalate if it doesn't clear or has been heat-set.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the linen is dry-clean-only, structured, lined, embellished, vintage, or unstable-dye; if the hidden-area test shows color transfer; if the garment has been through a hot wash or dryer; or if two conservative passes do not clear it. Escalate to a professional dry cleaner in any of those cases.
warnings: Use cool or lukewarm water unless the care label specifically permits warmer washing; avoid hot water in the Home path for uncertain, colored, structured, or high-value linen
Never tumble-dry residual oil — heat sets the stain
Never use chlorine bleach on colored linen
Do not rub or scrub — pushes oil into the weave
Pro-strength oxidizers, solvents, and bleaches are not Home steps

neverDo: Use cool or lukewarm water unless the care label specifically permits warmer washing; avoid hot water in the Home path for uncertain, colored, structured, or high-value linen
Never tumble-dry a linen garment with residual butter — heat sets the oil
Never use chlorine bleach on colored linen
Do not wet-treat structured, lined, embellished, dry-clean-only, or high-value linen at home unless the care label permits and construction is stable; route to a professional when uncertain
Never apply professional-strength oxidizers, solvents, or acids in the Home path
fiberSensitivities: Linen is cellulose (flax); colored linen requires controlled temperature and a colorfastness check
Structured, lined, embellished, or vintage linen may warp, distort, shrink, bleed, or lose finish under wet treatment; route to a professional
Use cool or lukewarm water unless the care label specifically permits warmer washing; high heat is not in the Home path for uncertain, colored, structured, or high-value linen
chemicalBoundaries: Home path: mild liquid laundry detergent and water only
No peroxide, oxygen bleach, or chlorine bleach as a default Home step on linen
Pro path may use controlled oxidizer chemistry only when care label permits, colorfastness is confirmed, and SDS-driven PPE / ventilation are in place
homeAllowed: Cool or lukewarm water per care label
Clean white cloth and dull edge for mechanical lift
Air-drying flat and visual inspection before any heat
proOnly: Concentrated surfactants, solvents, and oxidizers used in a controlled professional environment with PPE and ventilation per product SDS
testFirst: Hidden-area colorfastness test (white cloth on an inside seam) before any detergent dwell on colored linen
flushNeutralizeDry: Rinse thoroughly after each detergent application
Air-dry flat and inspect before any heat
escalationTriggers: Care label is dry-clean-only or restricts wet treatment
Linen is structured / lined / embellished / vintage / high-value
Stain has already been through a hot wash or dryer
Two conservative passes do not clear the stain
ppeVentilation: Follow the product label for any concentrate
Gloves and eye protection for any pro-strength chemistry
Ventilate the work area; keep cleaning agents away from children and pets
safetyMatrix.homeVsProBoundary: Home users use only mild liquid laundry detergent and care-label cool / lukewarm laundering. Pro chemistry (concentrated oxidizers, solvents) is hidden from the Home path and requires SDS-driven PPE / ventilation in a controlled environment.

materialWarnings: Avoid hot water in the Home path for uncertain, colored, structured, or high-value linen unless the care label specifically permits warmer washing
Never tumble-dry residual oil — heat sets the stain
Structured / lined linen is a pro case from the start
Hidden-area colorfastness test on colored linen before detergent

escalation.when: Dry-clean-only, structured, lined, embellished, vintage, or unstable-dye linen; colored linen that fails a hidden-area test; stain that has been through a hot wash or dryer; or no improvement after two conservative passes.
escalation.whatToTell: "Butter on [simple / structured / colored / vintage] linen, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with liquid laundry detergent and a cool care-label wash, air-dried flat. [Hot wash or dryer applied? — yes / no]."

contextQuestions: Is the linen plain and washable, or structured / lined / embellished / dry-clean-only?
How old is the stain — fresh, overnight, or older?
Has the garment been through a hot wash or dryer since the spill?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Simple flat linen with a washable care label is the only Home candidate. Anything structured, lined, embellished, vintage, or with unstable dye is a professional case from the start. Compound butters (garlic, herb) add pigment that may need additional pro treatment beyond the conservative Home steps.

sources.title: University Stain Science (compiled extension reference)
sources.evidence: Oil-family classification; conservative unknown-stain fallback (cool water, liquid detergent, rinse, air-dry); blot rather than rub; do not iron or dry stained fabric until removal is confirmed.

sources.title: EPA Alternative Cleaning Curriculum
sources.evidence: Surfactant chemistry for oily soils; care-label gate; product-label and PPE / ventilation requirements for concentrates.

sources.title: GONR Material Safety Matrix v2
sources.evidence: Linen fiber tolerances and limits; controlled temperature for colored linen; chlorine bleach only for white / colorfast linen; lipase / fat use cases respond to surfactant action; absolute safety gates including no cleaner-mixing.
